title: Pipe and Tubular Products: Manufacturing and Evaluation
narrationScript: Pipe carries everything the other product forms taught us, rolled into a cylinder, plus a manufacturing split that drives the whole inspection. A tube is either seamless, pierced and worked from a solid billet, or welded, formed from strip or plate and joined along a seam. Each route plants its own discontinuities, and nearly all of them run along the length of the pipe. That single fact explains the standard examination, a shear wave chasing around the circumference, because a beam traveling circumferentially meets a longitudinal discontinuity broadside. Seamless first. A heated round billet is spun between barrel shaped rolls that grip it and drive it forward over a piercing point. The metal actually tears open ahead of the plug. That is the Mannesmann effect. The pierced shell is then elongated over a mandrel or plug and sized to final dimensions. The rotary action means seamless defects often spiral. Laps on the inside or outside surface trace shallow helical paths. Billet seams and stringers stretch out along the axis. The piercing plug can score the bore, and the process can leave the wall eccentric, thicker on one side than the other. Welded pipe inherits strip and plate problems plus a weld seam. In electric resistance welding, strip is rolled into a can and the edges are heated by induction and forged together, a solid state joint with no filler metal. Its characteristic defects are lack of fusion, called cold weld, small unbonded spots called penetrators, and hook cracks, where stringers in the strip turn upward at the weld upset and crack near the bond line. All of these stand roughly vertical in the weld plane and run along the pipe axis. Submerged arc welded pipe, longitudinal or spiral, behaves like any fusion weld, with slag, porosity, lack of fusion, and cracks, plus laminations carried in from the parent plate. Now the examination. Standardization is built on notches. You take a piece of the same pipe, machine a longitudinal notch on the outside surface and another on the inside surface, commonly five percent of wall thickness deep with a small minimum, and use them to set up the test. The beam is a refracted shear wave aimed circumferentially. It enters the wall, bounces between the inner and outer surfaces in a vee path, and the notches tell you where in that path an inside or outside surface defect will appear. The inside notch shows up at the half vee position, the outside notch at the full vee. You adjust gain so both notch responses meet the procedure, equalize them as closely as the specification demands, and mark their screen positions. Then you scan in both circumferential directions, clockwise and counterclockwise, because a tilted defect favors one direction, and if the specification covers transverse defects you add scans along the axis in both directions too. Mills do this at speed, rotating the pipe under fixed transducers or spinning transducer heads around the pipe, usually in immersion or with water columns. In the field you do it by hand with a wedge contoured to the pipe. Curvature sets a limit worth memorizing. A shear beam launched at the outside surface travels a straight chord, and its closest approach to the pipe center equals the outside radius times the sine of the refracted angle. Make the wall too heavy for the diameter and a forty five degree beam never touches the bore. It skips along the outer wall and the inside surface goes untested. As a working rule, when wall thickness climbs past roughly fifteen percent of the outside diameter, a forty five degree beam is in danger of missing the bore, and practices flag heavy wall pipe for lower refracted angles, examination from both surfaces, or added straight beam coverage. Evaluation is refreshingly direct in tubular work. The reference notch defines the rejection level. Any indication that equals or exceeds the response from the reference notch is rejectable or must be investigated, and smaller recording levels may apply below that. Add a straight beam check for laminations in the pipe body and at weld lands, plus a wall thickness verification, and you have the full package. Around the circumference for longitudinal defects, along the axis for transverse ones, notches to set the yardstick, both directions every time. That is pipe testing in one breath, and the rest of this lesson fills in the details.
imagePrompt: A technician guides an angle-beam wedge around the circumference of a large-diameter steel pipe resting on turning rolls, water couplant streaking the surface and a flaw detector hanging from a shoulder strap.

sections.heading: Two Families: Seamless and Welded
sections.body:
Every tubular product is built one of two ways, and the route decides what you hunt for.

**Seamless** pipe starts as a solid round billet. In **rotary piercing**, barrel-shaped cross rolls spin the hot billet and drive it over a piercing plug; the metal ruptures ahead of the plug — the **Mannesmann effect** — and opens into a hollow shell. The shell is elongated over a **mandrel or plug mill** (or by **pilgering**), then sized or stretch-reduced to final dimensions. Stainless and nonferrous tube is often **extruded** instead. Cold drawing may finish the tube for gauge and surface.

**Welded** pipe begins as flat product. **ERW/HFW** pipe forms strip into a can and forges the induction-heated edges together — a solid-state weld with no filler metal, later trimmed flush. **SAW** pipe is fusion-welded from plate with filler, either as a longitudinal seam on formed cans or as a continuous **spiral** seam from coil. **Continuous (furnace) butt-welded** pipe is the low-grade end of the family.

The inspection consequence: seamless pipe carries **body** discontinuities distributed around its full circumference, while welded pipe concentrates its risk in and beside one **seam** — plus whatever the parent strip or plate brought along.

sections.caption: Seamless pipe carries helical laps and axial seams from rotary piercing, while ERW pipe concentrates lack of fusion, penetrators, and hook cracks along its longitudinal weld line.

sections.heading: What Each Route Leaves Behind
sections.body:
**Seamless:** the rotary action twists defects into shallow **helical paths**. Expect **laps** on the OD or ID following that spiral, **seams** and **stringers** stretched along the axis from billet defects, **plug scores** (axial gouges in the bore from a worn piercing point), and **eccentric wall** — thickness varying around the circumference. Transverse discontinuities are comparatively rare.

**ERW/HFW:** the bond line is a vertical plane running the pipe length. Its defects stand in that plane: **cold weld** (lack of fusion where the forged edges never bonded), **penetrators** (small spot-like unbonded windows), and **hook cracks** — stringers in the strip that turned upward in the weld upset and cracked alongside the bond line. Add flash-trim defects and edge laminations from the parent strip.

**SAW (straight or spiral):** conventional fusion-weld discontinuities — slag, porosity, lack of fusion or penetration, centerline cracks — distributed along a straight or helical seam, plus **laminations** from the parent plate.

The shared theme: **almost everything runs longitudinally** (or along the seam), standing roughly normal to the pipe surface. Transverse defects exist but are the minority. Orientation drives the scan plan.

sections.heading: Circumferential Shear-Wave Scanning
sections.body:
A discontinuity standing along the pipe axis is broadside to a beam traveling **around the circumference** — so the standard examination launches a refracted **shear wave** (commonly about 45°) circumferentially into the wall. The beam bounces between OD and ID in a **V-path**: a reflector at the **ID** appears at the **half-V** soundpath, one at the **OD** at the **full-V**. For 45° in a 12 mm wall, the half-V soundpath is 12 ÷ cos 45° ≈ **17 mm**.

Scan **both circumferential directions** — clockwise and counterclockwise — because a tilted or hooked defect reflects far better from one side than the other. Where the specification covers transverse discontinuities, add **axial scans in both directions** along the pipe.

Mills run this at production speed: the pipe spins under fixed transducers or a rotating head spins around the pipe, coupled by immersion or water columns, with gated alarms flagging indications. Manual examination uses a **wedge contoured to the pipe** — common practice contours the wedge whenever the gap under a flat shoe exceeds about 0.5 mm — with the probe walked around the circumference in an overlapping pattern. Either way, coverage must overlap and standardization must be re-verified at intervals and after any system change.

sections.caption: A circumferential shear-wave V-path strikes the ID notch at half-V and the OD notch at full-V; in heavy-wall pipe the 45-degree chord can miss the bore entirely.

sections.heading: Curvature Limits and Heavy Wall
sections.body:
In a curved wall the beam travels a straight **chord**, and simple geometry sets a hard limit. The chord's closest approach to the pipe center is **R · sin θ**, where R is the outside radius and θ the refracted angle. The beam reaches the bore only while R · sin θ is less than the inside radius — that is, while **sin θ < 1 − 2t/OD**.

Work the 45° case: sin 45° ≈ 0.707, so the beam stops reaching the ID once **t/OD exceeds about 0.15**. Drop to a 38° refracted angle (sin ≈ 0.616) and the limit stretches to about 0.19. That is why practices flag **heavy-wall pipe** — commonly around t/OD of 0.15 to 0.2 and above — for **lower refracted angles**, examination from **both surfaces**, or supplemental straight-beam coverage.

Two subtler curvature effects: the angle at which the beam strikes the ID grows steeper than the nominal wedge angle as the wall thickens, so ID-notch sensitivity fades **before** the geometric cutoff; and the curved entry surface spreads the beam compared with flat plate. The ID notch in your reference standard is the honest witness for both — if you cannot get a workable ID-notch response during standardization, the technique does not reach the bore, whatever the wedge label says.

sections.heading: Notch Standards, Acceptance, and Codes
sections.body:
Tubular standardization is built on a **reference standard cut from the same pipe** — same size, grade, and surface condition. Machined into it:

- A **longitudinal notch on the OD** and another **on the ID** (square, V, or buttress profile per the governing document), depth commonly **5% of nominal wall** with a small minimum (around 0.1 mm / 0.004 in.) and a stated maximum; length per specification.
- Where required: **transverse notches** for axial scanning, and sometimes a radially drilled hole.

Standardize by maximizing each notch response, **equalizing OD and ID responses** within the dB tolerance the procedure allows, marking their screen positions, and setting scanning gain. Production systems verify standardization dynamically — at speed — and re-verify at set intervals and after any change; a failed re-verification voids everything scanned since the last good check.

Acceptance is notch-referenced: any indication **equal to or exceeding the reference-notch response** is rejectable or must be investigated, with lower recording levels sometimes applied. Practice documents of the ASTM E213 pattern, product specifications in the A450/A530 families, and API line-pipe standards all follow this architecture, typically adding a **straight-beam lamination check** of the pipe body or weld-land zones and **wall-thickness verification**. As always, the purchase order names the governing document — read it before you cut notches.

sections.caption: Standardized ERW seam-scan screen with the ID and OD notch reference positions marked and an unexplained echo arriving before the half-V position.

sections.title: The notch is the yardstick
sections.body: Tubular acceptance is **notch-referenced**: equal or exceed the reference-notch response and the indication is rejectable or must be investigated. Notch depth is commonly **5% of wall** with small minimum and maximum limits. Equalize ID and OD responses during standardization, mark both screen positions, and re-verify at intervals and after any change — a failed check voids everything scanned since the last good one.

figures.title: Pipe manufacturing routes and their defects
Circumferential shear wave in the pipe wall
ERW seam-scan A-scan with notch references

interactive.title: Read the Seam-Scan Screen
interactive.intro: You standardized on ID and OD notches and marked their screen positions. Scanning the ERW seam, a new echo pops up. Click the indication that represents a mid-wall lack of fusion in the weld line.

regions.label: Initial pulse and wedge noise
regions.feedback: That is the initial pulse and wedge noise — present on every screen and carrying no metal-path information.

regions.label: Echo before the half-V position
regions.feedback: Correct. A reflector at mid-wall depth on the first leg returns a soundpath shorter than the half-V ID reference — the classic position for mid-wall lack of fusion (cold weld) in an ERW seam.

regions.label: Echo at the half-V position
regions.feedback: That soundpath matches the ID-notch reference — an ID-connected reflector such as an ID notch or ID-surface defect, not a mid-wall one.

regions.label: Echo at the full-V position
regions.feedback: That matches the OD-notch reference at full V — an OD-surface reflector beyond the weld, not a mid-wall indication.

checkQuestions.question: Hook cracks in ERW pipe originate from:
options: Hydrogen absorbed during induction heating
Stringers in the parent strip that turn upward in the weld upset and crack alongside the bond line
Excessive piercing-plug wear during rotary piercing
Thermal shock when the flash is trimmed
checkQuestions.explanation: The forging upset at the ERW bond line bends the strip's elongated inclusions upward; these upturned stringers crack adjacent to the weld, running axially — which is why they answer a circumferential shear-wave scan.

checkQuestions.question: Why is the primary pipe-body and seam examination performed with a shear wave traveling circumferentially?
options: Shear waves travel faster around curves than longitudinal waves
Most tubular discontinuities — laps, seams, ERW weld defects — run longitudinally, so a circumferential beam strikes them broadside
Circumferential scanning eliminates the need for couplant
The ID can only be reached by a circumferential beam
checkQuestions.explanation: Manufacturing elongates nearly everything along the pipe axis or seam line; a beam chasing the circumference meets those reflectors at close to normal incidence, maximizing response.

checkQuestions.question: A 45-degree shear wave examines a 12 mm pipe wall circumferentially. The half-V soundpath to the ID surface is about:
options: 12 mm
8.5 mm
17 mm
24 mm
checkQuestions.explanation: Half-V soundpath = T ÷ cos θ = 12 ÷ cos 45° = 12 ÷ 0.707 ≈ 17 mm. The full-V path back to the OD doubles it to about 34 mm.

checkQuestions.question: On heavy-wall pipe with t/OD ≈ 0.18, a standard 45-degree circumferential examination is inadequate because:
options: The shear wave mode-converts to a surface wave in thick walls
Attenuation in thick walls absorbs the full-V path
The beam's closest approach to the center (R·sin 45°) exceeds the bore radius, so the ID is never examined
The notch standard cannot be machined in heavy wall
checkQuestions.explanation: The chord's closest approach is R·sin θ; at 45° the beam stops reaching the ID once t/OD passes about 0.15, so at 0.18 it skips along the outer wall — a lower refracted angle or dual-surface technique is required.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement matches common tubular notch-standardization practice?
options: One OD notch is sufficient since ID and OD respond identically
Longitudinal OD and ID notches, commonly 5% of wall deep with a small minimum, are equalized and any indication equal to or exceeding the notch response is rejectable or must be investigated
Notches are cut 50% of wall deep to guarantee detection
Notch standards may be any convenient pipe size as long as the grade matches
checkQuestions.explanation: The reference standard comes from the same pipe size and grade with OD and ID longitudinal notches around 5% of wall deep; equalized notch responses define the screen positions and the rejection yardstick.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §2.4 (pipe and tubular products: manufacturing, discontinuities, response, codes/standards)
Angle-beam tube practices and notch standards per documents such as ASTM E213 and the A450/A530 product-specification families (cited generically)
API line-pipe and related service codes requiring seam examination (cited generically)
